Saints.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>U.S. District Court</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>For example, “Defendants adopted a blue MORMON STORIES logo with a light-rays design prominently used by the Church, showing Defendants’ intent to capitalize on and increase confusion.”</p><p>“The Church does not seek, in any way, to influence the content of Defendants’ podcast, but Defendants should not be allowed to use the Church’s trademarks or other Church intellectual property to cause confusion as to the source, affiliation, connection, endorsement, or authorization of Defendants’ podcast and other content.”</p><p>Prior to the lawsuit, Dehlin and the church were involved in mediation and Dehlin said he had agreed to almost all requests, except for what he described as an over-the-top disclaimer that he’s not affiliated with the church.</p><p>Dehlin said since the suit was filed, he’s been subjected to ugly comments online, including some that call him “Satan.”</p><p>He was excommunicated by the church in 2015 after speaking in favor of female ordination to the priesthood and same-sex marriage — though church officials have pointed to other factors, including statements “that the Book of Mormon and the Book of Abraham are fraudulent and works of fiction.”</p><h3>The lawsuit filed by the church</h3><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe src="https://docs.google.com/viewer?embedded=true&url=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/9f/be/7e4786aa475f84bacfe0aca4d518/lds-lawsuit-john-dehlin.pdf"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Robert Herguth

entries.title: Review: Chicago Shakes’ ‘Fault’ has mild amusements and noble performances but lacks conviction
entries.summary: <p>Despite its contemporary setting, the infrequently funny comedy “Fault,” receiving its world premiere at Chicago Shakespeare under the direction of Jason Alexander, feels like a play from a different era.</p><p>In its opening moments, when Jerry (Enrico Colantoni) enters his luxe Manhattan home to discover his wife, Lucy (Rebecca Spence), having sex with the younger Shaun (Nick Marini), “Fault” conjures the momentary spirit of a British sex farce. But that anarchic physical energy dissipates pretty much as soon as Sean runs into a door frame.</p><p>From then on, the play resembles nothing so much as an effort to revive the old-fashioned drawing room comedy. The widely popular, albeit wide-ranging genre, reaching its zenith in the first half of the 20th century, featured characters of great sophistication and wealth engaging in fast-paced, endlessly witty banter, preferably over cocktails.</p><div class="RatingCard Enhancement" data-module data-rating="2.5" data-align-center> <div class="RatingCard-title"> ‘Fault’ </div> <div class="RatingCard-content"> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> </div> </div><p></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-f50000" name="module-f50000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>When:</b> Through May 24<br><b>Where:</b> Chicago Shakespeare Theater, 800 E. Grand Ave.<br><b>Tickets:</b> $60-$126<br><b>Info:</b> <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagoshakes.com/" target="_blank" >chicagoshakes.com</a><br><b>Run time:</b> 1 hour and 30 minutes with no intermission</div> </div><p>Although there were prominent American purveyors — we saw a recent, worthier effort at breathing new life into the form with <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2026/02/12/review-the-goodman-holiday" target="_blank" >Richard Greenberg’s rewrite of Philip Barry’s “Holiday” at the Goodman</a> — the drawing room comedy was primarily a British concoction, with Noel Coward as its most famous master.</p><p>Not for nothing does writer Scooter Pietsch make Jerry an avowed Anglophile, and the opulent set here from Paul Tate dePoo III (who also designed <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2026/04/24/review-great-gatsby-musical-broadway-chicago-theater-things-to-do" target="_blank" >“The Great Gatsby”</a> tour production, currently in town until May 3) could come from a Coward play — it’s plastered with seemingly centuries-old British landscapes. Some early dialogue even sparked memories of the first two scenes of Coward’s “Private Lives,” where a divorced couple, each now newly remarried and honeymooning at the same hotel, encourage their second spouse’s understanding that the marital failure was all the other’s fault.</p><p>The setup here is even more wholly manufactured then basically ignored. Jerry, a big-time merger and acquisitions broker who has closed the deal of his life before he walks in, decides that he and Lucy — “a cutthroat lawyer” — will compete to determine who is most at fault for the current state of their marriage. Shaun — baffled, bloody-nosed and handcuffed to a chair — will be the judge. The task is so unpleasant that, in addition to the handcuffs, Jerry has to offer him scads of money to stay.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b00000" name="image-b00000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/216c242/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7339x4119+0+388/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8d%2F4d%2F2943d02d46d79f4dffa670541fde%2F1-fault-cst-justin-barbin.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b9ad797/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7339x4119+0+388/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8d%2F4d%2F2943d02d46d79f4dffa670541fde%2F1-fault-cst-justin-barbin.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Enrico Colantoni (right) and Nick Marini star in Chicago Shakespeare’s production of “Fault.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Justin Barbin</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>OK, it’s a thin concoction, but “Fault” would be far better if Pietsch committed and executed. He doesn’t. Neither character makes any compelling case that the other is guilty of much beyond an affair and one difference of opinion on a business deal a decade ago, and instead, we get all defense and no prosecution. This should be a game of one-upmanship. There’s no up.</p><p>“Fault” has mild amusements and noble performances, but it’s stuck in a low comic gear that shifts tones without ever finding an authentic one. The physical comedy early settles into verbal jabs, then a couple of sentimental monologues (Pietsch’s most effective writing actually, revealing the poor rich people’s insecurities about aging), then an effort at pure black comedy that feels at odds with what came before.</p><p>The production under Alexander’s direction has a good forward-moving thrust, keeping up the pace, but pace can’t make up for bland repartee that relies on timing rather than cleverness.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-280000" name="image-280000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d93a469/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7371x4137+0+390/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9b%2Fdd%2Ffa99be2743b2a6fbedf16eb16d75%2F2-fault-cst-justin-barbin.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rebecca Spence portrays Lucy and Nick Marini is Shaun in Chicago Shakespeare’s production of “Fault.” </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Justin Barbin</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Fortunately, good timing is this production’s biggest plus.</p><p>Colantoni, best known for his TV and film work, possesses an intuitive sense of timing and a seemingly effortless way of showing two contradictory emotions at once on his face, doing battle with each other. His performance in the “Star Trek” homage “Galaxy Quest” — as the benevolent but confused alien leader — was the heart of the 1999 movie that created all the stakes to make the comedy work, and that’s saying a lot for a cast that included Alan Rickman and Sigourney Weaver.</p><p>He’s entertaining to watch in “Fault,” but he’s also miscast, never believable as a wannabe patrician, and the role is too one-note to allow him the opportunity to do what he does best.</p><p>Spence, who replaced Teri Hatcher at the start of rehearsals (she left for “personal reasons”), comes closer to right for the role. She’s also a performer who has notably shined among a star cast (as one of the title characters in “Mary Page Marlowe” opposite Blair Brown and Carrie Coon), and she brings a grounded honesty, particularly in a climactic monologue.</p><p>I’m not as familiar with Marini’s work (Netflix’s “Cobra Kai”), but he makes the most of a badly underwritten role. If there’s one thing Pietsch could do to improve this play quickly, it would be to make Shaun a livelier foil from the start. We aren’t in the era anymore of the drawing room comedies that poked gentle and admiring fun at the upper classes. Perhaps fleshing out the outsider here might provide a different path into more authentically acerbic humor.</p>
entries.author: Steven Oxman | For the Sun-Times

entries.title: Chicago Gourmet 2026 tickets go on sale Wednesday
entries.summary: <p>Tickets go on sale Wednesday for Chicago Gourmet, the city’s “premier culinary festival,” taking place Sept. 24-27 on the Harris Theater Rooftop at Millennium Park.</p><p>Chicago Gourmet “is a premier culinary festival here in Chicago,” said Sam Toia, president and CEO of the Illinois Restaurant Association, which produces the multi-day event. “We are the culinary capital of the United States, and Chicago Gourmet puts a spotlight on that.”</p><p>Ticket sales open on International Dance Day, a nod to this year’s theme “Let Your Tastebuds Dance.” Performances such as a live breakdance battle, salsa and folklore dancing will be paired with tacos, burgers and tasting portions dished up by chefs over the four-day festival.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Tacos & Tequila will kick off Chicago Gourmet on Sept. 24.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Kevin Eduard White</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Returning this year are live demonstrations by chefs, which had been eliminated due to the pandemic, Toia said. The fan-favorite demos will be held Sept. 26 at the Pritzker Pavilion stage. Guests with Grand Cru tickets can add a demo ticket to see chefs Stephanie Izard (Girl & the Goat), Sarah Grueneberg (Monteverde) and Rick Bayless (Frontera Grill, Topolobampo) in action.</p><p>Among new participants this year include chefs from restaurants specializing in Oaxacan fare: Leo Ramos and Reynel Mendoza of Kie-Gol-Lanee, which has a location in Logan Square and Uptown; and Carlos Munoz of Istmo in Lake View.</p><p>Also joining the lineup this year are chefs Todd Hawkesworth of River North tavern Gus' Sip & Dip, which garnered Bar of the Year in this <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/food-drink/2026/01/25/banchet-awards-ceremony-winners-chicago-chefs-restaurants" target="_blank" >year’s Banchet Awards</a>; and Brian Lockwood, who served as culinary consultant on Hulu show “The Bear,” of Gingie in River North. Every year, Chicago Gourmet features chefs from around the country, and this year “Top Chef” contestant chef Lee Anne Wong from Koko Head Cafe in Honolulu as well as James Beard Award winner Chris Shepherd of Southern Smoke Foundation in Houston, Texas, will make appearances.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9db19c4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7360x4131+0+391/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F68%2F51%2F0547da6f4901aa7f1f5761c4e457%2F2025-09-26-chicago-gourmet-hamburger-hop-event-4940.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Hamburger Hop is a burger battle among chefs at Chicago Gourmet.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Kevin Eduard White</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The multi-day festival kicks off on Sept. 24 with Tacos and Tequila hosted by chefs Rick Bayless, Marcos Carbajal (Carnitas Uruapan) and Diana Davila (Mi Tocaya Antojería). The following night, Hamburger Hop pits chefs against each other as they battle for burger supremacy. Chefs Joe Flamm (Rose Mary, il Carciofo, Bar Tutto and BLVD Steakhouse) and Jeff Mauro (Mauro Provisions and co-host of Food Network’s “The Kitchen”) will host the burger face-off to determine the Judges’ Choice and People’s Choice.</p><p>“Hamburger Hop is such a great opportunity for chefs from all over the city to showcase their grilling skills, push creativity, and just have fun with it,” Flamm said. “Chicago Gourmet has been a constant throughout my career, and it’s an honor to be back hosting and celebrating the city’s incredible culinary community.”</p><p>On Saturday, Grand Cru, featuring food from 30-plus chefs, will take place across two sessions, with Stephanie Izard hosting the afternoon fete and Sarah Grueneberg and Chris Shepherd taking over in the evening. The festival culminates with Southern-inspired brunch Rise & Shine Gourmet, hosted by chefs Art Smith (Reunion) and Erick Williams (Virtue Restaurant & Bar, Mustard Seed Kitchen and Daisy’s Po-Boy and Tavern).</p><p>Launched in 2008, Chicago Gourmet comes as the industry is still reeling from the pandemic while facing increased food and labor costs. “I always say that, before the pandemic, the restaurant industry was an industry of nickels and dimes. Now it's turned into an industry of pennies and nickels,” Toia said.</p><p>“So it's tough. But people that are in the industry, they love it. They love being chefs. They're artists,” Toia said of chefs who are dedicated to their craft.</p><p>Proceeds benefit the Illinois Restaurant Association Educational Foundation. Working with nearly 60 high schools across the state, including 20 in Chicago, Chicago Gourmet “benefits the future stars of the industry,” Toia said. The culinary event also benefits the Restaurant Employee Relief Fund, which supports industry workers facing personal challenges such as an accident, illness and death of a family member.</p><p>Tickets range from $115 for Rise & Shine and $130 for Tacos & Tequila and Hamburger Hop. Tickets for Grand Cru are $255.</p><p>For more information, go to <a class="Link" href="http://chicagogourmet.org" target="_blank" ><u>chicagogourmet.org</u></a>.</p>
entries.author: Dorothy Hernandez

entries.title: Chicago Critics Film Festival to feature Olivia Wilde, Chicago indie filmmaker Joe Swanberg
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Critics Film Festival is unique in the cinema scene. The annual week of screenings at the Music Box Theatre is curated exclusively by film critics. Arguably the city’s most selective festival, it is the city’s smallest for a reason. The simplified schedule books only one feature or shorts program at a time. Trust us, the critics are saying, we want you to see everything.</p><p>Among the highlights is the idiosyncratically punctuated “The Invite,” which kicks off the festival Friday. Director and star Olivia Wilde is scheduled to attend the 6:30 p.m. screening of a 35 mm print. She plays Angela, the wife of Joe (Seth Rogen). Their marriage is shaky, so they figure now is the best time to invite the dodgy couple upstairs, portrayed by Penelope Cruz and Edward Norton. What could go wrong? Judging by the trailer, since the film was unavailable for preview, the get-together goes sideways. This screening is sold out, but the film, from ace distributor A24, hits select theaters on June 26.</p><p>Members of the Chicago Film Critics Association pick films from out-of-town festivals they covered for their respective outlets. “The Invite” premiered in January at the Sundance Film Festival in Park City, Utah. Centerpiece documentary “When a Witness Recants” also premiered at Sundance. Concluding the festival on May 7, “The Sun Never Sets” by Chicago indie auteur Joe Swanberg premiered in March at South By Southwest film festival in Austin, Texas.</p><p>The festival, founded in April 2013, began as a way to showcase the Chicago Film Critics Association, said Brian Tallerico, co-producer, website coordinator, film critics association board president and managing editor of <a class="Link" href="http://rogerebert.com" target="_blank" ><u>RogerEbert.com</u></a>.</p><p>“In the years before the festival, we did a Chicago Film Critics Association Awards, and those were getting harder to put on, to get celebrities to come,” said Tallerico, who is from Arlington Heights. “Things changed after 9/11.” </p><p>What to do next? “A forward-facing program that would highlight the CFCA,” he added.</p><p>Initially held at suburban Muvico Rosemont 18, the festival was “like an experiment,” said Erik Childress, co-producer of the festival and board member who reviews on the "Movie Madness" podcast" and reports on the box office at Rotten Tomatoes.</p><p>Childress and Tallerico lucked out for the inaugural event. For their first opening night, they screened “Stories We Tell” by Canadian actor/director Sarah Polley, who made an appearance. Another big name, Chicago native William Friedkin, director of “The Exorcist” and “The French Connection,” also stopped by. On a book tour for his memoir “The Friedkin Connection,” he brought a precious 35 mm print of his 1977 action thriller “Sorcerer.”</p><p>The debut Chicago Critics Film Festival was dedicated to the late Roger Ebert. The Chicago Sun-Times critic’s Overlooked Film Festival and EbertFest served “as a sort of a model, as someone who was taking his art and his passion and his interest, and using it to showcase films,” noted Tallerico. Ebert founded and programmed that annual fest in 1999 in Champaign, Illinois. Its historic venue, the Virginia Theatre, was a 1920s-era movie palace like the Music Box.</p><p>European film festivals early in the year traditionally supply features that travel to North America’s big festivals in the fall. Tallerico said when it comes to curating films for the fest, “quality control” is paramount.</p><p>“We have turned down films that I know would have made us money because we didn't like the film. Quality control is above and beyond everything that we care about. But increasingly, [other studios are] picking things up at Sundance and South by Southwest and dropping them in May and June. So we've tried to fill in a space. We are where we are to kind of maximize the buzz. Roughly half our program every year premiered at Sundance or South By Southwest.”</p><p>Retrospective screenings this year are projected on 35 mm: It's the 40th anniversary of David Cronenberg’s “The Fly,” the 25th anniversary of Steven Spielberg’s timely “A.I. Artificial Intelligence” and Jonathan Demme’s “Something Wild” from 1986.</p><p>Chicago Critics Film Festival will co-present select screenings with Black Women Directors, Instituto Cervantes de Chicago and Italian Cultural Institute of Chicago’s Moto Continuo Film Showcase.</p><p>Here is a selection of films to check out during the festival. All will screen at the Music Box, 3733 N. Southport Ave. All are Chicago premieres. None played at earlier Chicago festivals.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f70000" name="image-f70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7514f35/2147483647/strip/true/crop/998x560+0+53/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F93%2Ff1%2F0f946585452498993d44768a88dd%2Fdecorado.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Decorado”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago Critics Film Festival</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b>‘Decorado’</b><br>Animator Alberto Vazquez directs the angsty saga of a mouse increasingly wary of Almighty Limitless Megacorporative Agency. Out of work with time on his hands, he researches whether he and his wife and everyone else dwell in a blue-pilled dystopic simulation. His doctor prescribes pills. Ingredients? “100% Happiness.”<br><i>12 a.m. Friday, part of Music Box’s Animation Adventures series</i></p><p><b>‘Shorts Program #1’ </b><br>In “Paper Trail,” Don Hertzfeldt animates a 14-minute life story via a revelatory collage of papers he saw and altered, from childish scrawls to faltering signatures, as retirement nears. Seven other shorts have remarkable unforeseen endings. Four filmmakers, though not Hertzfeldt, are coming for a Q&A.<br><i>11:30 a.m. Saturday</i></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-810000" name="image-810000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“You Had To Be There”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago Critics Film Festival</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b>‘You Had To Be There: How the Toronto Godspell Ignited the Comedy Revolution, Spread Love & Overalls, and Created a Community That Changed the World (in a Canadian Kind of Way)’ </b><br>The longest titled film is the most entertaining. Nick Davis revisits the 1972 Toronto production of a musical that boosted the careers of Martin Short, Eugene Levy, Gilda Radner, Victor Garber, Andrea Martin, Dave Thomas and Paul Shaffer. Sharing their recollections of youth and decades-long camaraderie is a gift.<br><i>11:30 a.m. Sunday</i></p><p><b>‘Shorts Program #2’ </b><br>Who can resist this trailer tagline: “See it so you can say you saw it!” Eric Jackowitz riffs on Italian gore cinema in campy spoof “The Seeing Eye Dog Who Saw Too Much.” Only the dog of a sexy blind violinist can see the killer. One of the six other shorts in this program ends with the disclaimer: “This film was not made with generative AI.” Four filmmakers will attend.<br><i>1:45 p.m. Sunday</i></p><div class="Enhancement" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“In ‘When A Witness Recants,’ the Harlem Park Three, who spent 36 years in prison after being wrongfully convicted of a Baltimore murder at 16, finally tell their story. Credit: </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sundance Institute</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b>‘When a Witness Recants’ </b><br>This compelling HBO documentary is about a witness who comes face to face, on camera, with three now-free men he helped imprison for 36 years. In 1983, a teenager’s untruthful testimony convicted three other teens for murdering a 14-year-old student in a Baltimore middle school hallway for his jacket. Director Dawn Porter will attend a don’t-miss Q&A after the screening.<br><i>6:30 p.m. Monday</i></p><p><b>‘Time and Water’ </b><br>Sara Dosa directs an elegiac ecological essay on Iceland’s disappearing glaciers. Icelandic author Andri Snær Magnason narrates. Family home movies interlace with artful shots of “living” glaciers and the abstract sounds they make. As Magnason’s glacier-loving grandparents age, their memories melt as well.<br><i>9:30 p.m. Monday</i></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5c432b8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/950x533+0+50/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3d%2F7b%2Faf2699a3418985f87e236da26082%2Fblack-zombie.jpg" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Black Zombie”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago Critics Film Festival</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b>‘Black Zombie’ </b><br>Maya Annik Bedward directs an incisive look into Haiti's colonial sugar plantations and the living tradition of voodoo. Spiritual forces liberated the enslaved Africans who worked to death for European wealth. What does that have to do with our freaky fixation on movie and TV zombies? Everything, argues this illuminating history lesson; the original zombies were oppressed workers struggling to be free and fully human. Die-hard fans of zombie cinema, prepare to get schooled.<br><i>9:45 p.m. Tuesday</i></p>
entries.author: Bill Stamets

entries.title: Gia Margaret lost her voice but found a new vulnerability
entries.summary: <p>The title of Gia Margaret’s new album “Singing” is more than a simple word or action – it’s a celebration of the Chicago singer’s nearly eight-year journey toward reclaiming her voice after her vocal chords were damaged.</p><p>“I think singing for me is so much more than just the act of it,” explained Margaret, 38. “I think singing is the ultimate vulnerability.”</p><div class="Form-newsletter-text-wrapper"> <div class="Form-newsletter-title"> Green Room Newsletter </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-submissionSuccess"> <p><b>You’re subscribed!</b></p><p></p><p>Please check your inbox for your confirmation.</p> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-description">Stay ahead of what’s hitting Chicago stages, galleries, museums and more with Green Room, WBEZ’s weekly arts & culture newsletter!</div> <div class="Form-newsletter-errorMessage"><p>Sorry, there was an error registering your email.</p></div> </div> <form data-ajax-endpoint="https://sfmc-subscribe.wbez.org/v1/subscription" class="Form-newsletter-form" method="POST" > <div class="Form-newsletter-item"> <div class="EmailInput"><label class="EmailInput-label"><span>Email</span> <input class="EmailInput-input" type="email" name="email" placeholder="name@email.com" required aria-required="true"> </label> </div> <button class="Button" type="submit">Sign Up </button> </div> <div class="Form-items-item"> <input type="hidden" name="acquisition_campaign" value="ARTS_Affordance"> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-disclaimer"> <p>By subscribing, you agree with WBEZ’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/terms-of-use" target="_blank" >Terms of Use</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/privacy-policy" target="_blank" >Privacy Policy</a></p> </div> </form> </bsp-form> </div> </div><p>It’s a quality she’s sought to showcase since her 2018 debut, “There’s Always A Glimmer,” which received rave reviews from national outlets such as Pitchfork. Primed for the next level, the artist was in talks with record label Jagjaguwar and already signed with Beggars Music for publishing. There were also plans for a headline tour and a follow-up album.</p><p>However, nerve damage to her vocal cords left her in pain whenever she tried to sing.</p><p>“I definitely was scared that my career was over and everything that I had worked for was finally coming to fruition,” she said. “It all felt like it was falling apart.”</p><p>Margaret ultimately turned to work as a producer and composer. She initially created her 2020 ambient album, “Gia Margaret,” out of boredom.</p><p>“I think that interest was always there, but I was kind of just thrown into it because I wanted to express myself through music in any way that I could,” said Margaret, who has always called the city home and studied music composition at Columbia College before pursuing songwriting.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-690000" name="module-690000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><b>Gia Margaret in concert</b></h3><b>Where</b>: Thalia Hall, 1807 S. Allport St<br><b>When</b>: May 12<br><b>Tickets</b>: Starting at $33.52<br><b>Info</b>: <a class="Link" href="http://thaliahallchicago.com" target="_blank" ><u>thaliahallchicago.com</u></a></div> </div><p>Her interest in exploring new forms of music blossomed, leading to the release of the instrumental “Romantic Piano” in 2023, utilizing some of the classical piano lessons she learned as a child. She said the project reaffirmed her love for music.</p><p>“It’s something that helps me process and stay grounded through whatever is going on in my life,” said Margaret, who also plays guitars, synthesizer, drums/percussion, flute, and keyboards on the new album.</p><p>The project also gave her confidence to do everything she could to bring her voice back. She took vocal therapy and voice lessons, and found that vocoders allowed her to better focus on melody and harmony without being overly self-critical. Eventually, she adjusted to an older-sounding voice that she almost didn’t recognize at first and accepted that she couldn’t push her voice as hard as she once had.</p><p>This process allowed Margaret to feel empowered to expand her musical skillset.</p><p>“I think this album felt way more vulnerable than the past two,” she said of her new collection of folk and pop-tinged songs.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="150" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/3tmOnwm4AzE?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Gia Margaret - Good Friend (Official Video)"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>Margaret will celebrate her album, released Friday via Jagjaguwar, with a release show May 12 at Thalia Hall. She will be joined by a trio of backing musicians, including longtime friend and collaborator Deb Talan, who contributed to the new album.</p><p>The album was recorded between 2024 and 2025 at studios in London, Eau Claire, Wisconsin, and Chicago with a plethora of collaborators including Kurt Vile, David Bazan, Sean Carey, and Guy Sigsworth.</p><p>It’s a combination that Talan feels led Margaret’s most dynamic and powerful album yet.</p><p>“There’s a depth, a maturity, and a richness that has expanded in what she does,” said Talan, former singer of the Chicago pop-folk band The Weepies. “I feel [her music] could appeal to anyone. It’s both melodic and lyrical, but then I feel like she creates these beautiful sonic environments that you just want to live in.”</p><p>They met about nine years ago when Talan asked Margaret to open for and sing with The Weepies. They’ve become close friends and collaborators – Talan says Margaret even “developed some really sweet relationships with my kids.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5a0000" name="image-5a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" alt="Gia Margaret" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8807364/2147483647/strip/true/crop/11656x6542+0+1100/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F28%2Fbbe02d634c0988630fdc8d09ae8a%2Fgia-25-213.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/489a719/2147483647/strip/true/crop/11656x6542+0+1100/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F28%2Fbbe02d634c0988630fdc8d09ae8a%2Fgia-25-213.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8807364/2147483647/strip/true/crop/11656x6542+0+1100/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F28%2Fbbe02d634c0988630fdc8d09ae8a%2Fgia-25-213.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Margaret will celebrate her album with a release show May 12 at Thalia Hall.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Graham Tolbert</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>While her friend typically has gentle guidance as a collaborator, Talan was amazed by her frequent use of the phrase “Be as freaky as you want.” It encouraged her to play a little more wildly than she normally would on album closer “E-Motion.”</p><p>“We ran my acoustic guitar through a sustained distortion pedal,” Talan explained. It goes from being pretty meditative, and then it has this part that gets really big and it's really fun.”</p><p>Margaret said she felt “a little more liberated to go off the deep end” with her voice, which now feels “way more experimental.” She went from writing songs that were minimal vocally to writing the longest song she had written to that point.</p><p>“It’s kind of like when you train for a marathon, you just try to go a little bit further each time. But you don't want to overdo it so that you can run the race eventually,” said Margaret. “There was a part of me that had to let go of some expectation that I had to come back and be this excellent singer. I was just really happy that I could do it at all.”</p>
entries.author: Joshua M. Miller

entries.title: Woman hurt in overnight Greater Grand Crossing blaze
entries.summary: <p>A woman was injured in a residential fire early Wednesday in the Greater Grand Crossing neighborhood on the South Side. </p><p>Chicago Fire Department officials responded to the 8000 block of South Ellis Avenue around 2:45 a.m. for the fire, a CFD spokesperson said. </p><p>One person was hospitalized in fair condition, officials said. </p><p>The cause of the fire was under investigation. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-d/" target="_blank" >Judge criticized for putting man later charged with shooting cops on electronic monitoring</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-h/" target="_blank" >Sophie Sherry</a></h5><p><b>Shooting suspect</b>: In December, Judge John Lyke Jr. placed Alphanso Talley, 26, of South Shore, on electronic monitoring after the convicted felon was charged with armed carjacking and armed robbery. Talley is now charged with killing Chicago police officer John Bartholomew and wounding another officer in an ambush attack at Endeavor Swedish Hospital on Saturday.</p><p><b>At issue</b>: Some officials have seized on Lyke’s decision to grant Talley leniency, saying it exposes the potentially fatal flaws of Cook County’s electronic monitoring program and the state’s cashless bail system. Advocates for bail reform argued that blame should primarily be placed on Lyke, not on the historic overhaul of the state’s criminal justice system.</p><p><b>What's next</b>: Talley, who on Tuesday was supposed to answer for the arrest warrants before Lyke, will face a full detention hearing Thursday.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-k/" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-u/" target="_blank" >Obamacare premiums in Illinois rose sharply but not as much as expected</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-o/" target="_blank" >Elvia Malagón</a></h5><p><b>Rise in payments</b>: Monthly premium payments for health insurance through the Affordable Care Act, commonly called Obamacare, have risen by about 25%. Nearly three months after open enrollment ended, providers say it’s still early but worry that increased healthcare costs could drive up insurance rates — and it all could lead to more uninsured patients.</p><p><b>Zooming in</b>: The household ACA monthly average premium is $328, up from $260 a year ago, according to state data. But if people buying coverage on the ACA exchange hadn’t adjusted their plans at all this year, the state expected the monthly average to rise to $464.</p><p><b>Listen up 🎧</b>: Reporter <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-b/" target="_blank" >Elvia Malagón appeared Friday on WBEZ’s talk show “Say More with Mary Dixon and Patrick Smith.”</a> Callers shared stories about navigating rising healthcare costs, including forgoing well-paying jobs to stay on Medicaid and shopping around for an affordable measles vaccine. Listen to the full episode by clicking below.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-n/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>ENVIRONMENT target="_blank" >Illinois state agencies at odds over endangered species protections</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-x/" target="_blank" >Juanpablo Ramirez-Franco</a></h5><p><b>Fish story</b>: Bigeye shiners, fish on the state’s endangered species list, have managed to survive despite habitat loss driven by decades of construction and industrial farm runoff. But an ongoing dispute between the Illinois Department of Transportation and the Illinois Department of Natural Resources over species protections is testing how the freshwater animal will endure.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: The standoff between IDOT and IDNR, outlined in internal state documents, is at the center of an ongoing clash that broke out last year after IDOT repeatedly ignored recommendations from state experts to pursue permits designed to protect imperiled species during road, bridge and other transportation work.</p><p><b>Bottom line</b>: The widening rift between the state’s largest public landowner and its top wildlife conservation agency shows how transportation projects may have overridden Illinois’ Endangered Species Protection Act in 11 cases in the past year.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-m/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>MORE NEWS YOU NEED 🗞️</h4><div class="Enhancement" target="_blank" >Chicago’s school board election is coming — here’s what to know, what’s at stake</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-jl/" target="_blank" >Emmanuel Camarillo</a></h5><p><b>Historic election</b>: On Nov. 3, voters will elect all 21 members of the Chicago School Board for the first time. It’s a significant shift for the governing body, which was under mayoral control for decades. The city held its first-ever school board elections in 2024, but that year, only 10 seats were up for grabs. The rest, including the board president, were appointed by Mayor Brandon Johnson.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: For years, advocates pushed for an elected board, arguing it would inject democracy and community voice into the board’s decision-making. But there are concerns that big money expected to flow in this year’s races will undermine that goal.</p><p><b>Stay informed</b>: We've broken down what you need to know about the board’s responsibilities and what to expect in the elections later this year <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-jr/" target="_blank" >here</a>.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-jy/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>SHARE YOUR QUESTIONS ✍️</h4><p>Whether you’re a Chicago Public Schools parent or taxpayer, the future of our city’s school system affects you.</p><p>That’s why WBEZ, the Sun-Times and Chalkbeat Chicago want to know what issues matter the most to you — and what questions you have for school board candidates.</p><p>Your responses will shape our community-driven elected school board voter guide, to be released this fall.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-jt/" target="_blank" ><b>TAKE OUR SURVEY</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>WATCH: LEARN THE SCHOOL BOARD ELECTION BASICS 🗳️</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="113" height="200" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/2aOiHPNU0U8?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Tell us your questions for Chicago’s school board candidates!"></iframe> </div> </div></div><hr><p></p><h4>ON WBEZ 91.5 FM 📻</h4><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-jh/" target="_blank" ><b>In the Loop with Sasha-Ann Simons, 9 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gia Margaret’s new album “Singing” was released last week.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Graham Tolbert</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-td/" target="_blank" >Chicago songwriter lost her voice but found new strength in vulnerability</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-th/" target="_blank" >Joshua M. Miller</a></h5><p>The title of Gia Margaret’s new album "Singing" is more than a word or action – it’s a celebration of the Chicago singer's nearly eight-year journey toward reclaiming her voice after her vocal cords were damaged.</p><p>"I think singing for me is so much more than just the act of it," explained Margaret, 38. "I think singing is the ultimate vulnerability."</p><p>It’s a quality she’s sought to showcase since her acclaimed 2018 debut, "There’s Always A Glimmer." Primed for the next level, the artist was in talks for a label deal. There were also plans for a headline tour and a follow-up album.</p><p>However, nerve damage to her vocal cords left her in pain whenever she tried to sing.</p><p>"I definitely was scared that my career was over and everything that I had worked for was finally coming to fruition," she said. "It all felt like it was falling apart."</p><p>After releasing two instrumental albums, years of vocal therapy and voice lessons, "Singing" is a triumph for Margaret and the start of a new chapter with a new voice.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-tk/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p><b>What aspect of buying tickets for big Chicago events like concerts and sports frustrates you the most, and what's your strategy for dealing with it?</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply to this newsletter</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</i></p><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-il/" target="_blank" >What’s your favorite springtime activity?</a></p><p>"Weeding!" — <i>Rhonda Peebles</i></p><p>"Having a plan B when we get together. It could be an alternate location or menu, based on daily or hourly weather changes." — <i>Christine Bock</i></p><p>"Seeing all the beautiful tulips displaying their diverse colors downtown, along suburban roads, or at a tulip farm/festival. After being dormant for many months, the bulbs coming back to life are so life-affirming!" — <i>Paul Lockwood</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia<br><b>Hat tip</b>: Sun-Times' Joel Carlson for today's subject line, which you'll find on the <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdrktlk-l-ir/" target="_blank" >front page of today’s newspaper</a> — on newsstands and online now.</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Sky extend Kamilla Cardoso, Rickea Jackson, and Jacy Sheldon
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> picked up fourth-year options for Kamilla Cardoso, Rickea Jackson, and Jacy Sheldon, according to the WNBA transactions page. All three players are entering their third year of their rookie contracts, which means the Sky had until May 15 to extend them a fourth year on team-friendly terms.</p><p>The move was expected, as this group now comprises the Sky’s young core. The Sky drafted Cardoso No. 3 in the 2024 draft and then traded for Jackson and Sheldon this offseason. They gave up <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/12/out-with-the-old-in-with-the-new-sky-trade-ariel-atkins-for-rickea-jackson-and-begin-again" >shooting guard Ariel Atkins to get Jackson from the Sparks</a>, and their own 2028 first-round pick to get Sheldon from the Mystics.</p><p>Cardoso, Jackson, and Sheldon were the No. 3, No. 4 and No. 5 picks in the 2024 draft, respectively.</p><p>The Sky also have the following players signed through the 2027 season: Skylar Diggins, Courtney Vandersloot, Azurá Stevens, Elizabeth Williams, and Gabriela Jaquez. </p><p>Should Hailey Van Lith, Maddy Westbeld, or any of the Sky's second or third-round draft picks make the final roster, they will also be on that list.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Davis Martin’s hot start to a potential breakout season is built on trust with White Sox
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> starter Davis Martin had already thrown 89 pitches and lost a tick on his fastball velocity on Tuesday night against the Angels, but he wanted that sixth inning.</p><p>Manager Will Venable might’ve called it a night for other arms on a young pitching staff that has produced uneven results early in the year — but not from Martin, who got two more outs en route to his <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/28/drew-romo-white-sox-two-home-runs-catcher" target="_blank" ><u>fourth win</u></a> of the season.</p><p>It’s a sign of trust the second-year skipper has in the veteran righty, who has put himself on the map over the first month of the season with a 1.95 ERA that was tied for eighth in MLB entering play Wednesday</p><p>And that trust is all Martin, 29, has ever wanted in a career that got a bumpy start out of the gate due to injuries.</p><p>“It’s everything. That’s what you want to do. You want to be dependable, you want to be relied on, you want to be the rock,” Martin said after his sixth rock-solid outing in a row to start the season. “[Venable] and I had a candid conversation, and that trust kind of builds up throughout the years, and so [I] just continue to do my job and be honest with him with where I’m at and go from there.”</p><p>Where Martin is at now is in the somewhat unexpected position of staff ace, following the early-season struggles and Triple-A demotion of Opening Day starter <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/24/demoted-starter-shane-smith-showing-improvement-in-charlotte" >Shane Smith</a>.</p><p>He’s also the only Sox starter aside from prized rookie Noah Schultz who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/06/grant-taylor-opener-white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>hasn’t gotten the opener treatment</u></a> from Venable & Co. to take the edge off early runs through opposing batting orders, working at least into the sixth inning in all but one of his starts.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c30000" name="image-c30000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6f8f495/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5534x3106+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2F79%2F57e13677418ea2cccd72eea967e3%2F2272833022.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3b8c9b4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5534x3106+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2F79%2F57e13677418ea2cccd72eea967e3%2F2272833022.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Davis Martin is congratulated by Munetaka Murakami after being removed from his start at Arizona earlier this month.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Christian Petersen/Getty</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Every outing he just builds confidence,” Venable said. “He’s getting great results, really from the first start. He just kind of felt like a guy out there that you can depend on throwing strikes, throwing deep in games, gives us a chance to win every single one of his starts.”</p><p>But Martin’s not just the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/16/white-sox-davis-martin-shane-smith-martin-perez-shea-langeliers-athletics" >de facto ace</a>. He’s honed in on his cutter and kick-changeup to round out a complete arsenal, stymying imposing lineups with stuff that’s effective if not flashy.</p><p>"No. 1, he's throwing a lot of strikes. We've talked about that but the stuff is really good too,” Venable said. “We've seen some really good sliders, good use of the cutter and throwing the four-seam and two-seam fastballs, so really mixing it up well and continuing to pound the zone.”</p><p>And with Martin also serving as the unofficial off-field captain of a tight-knit rotation, Venable hopes that’s rubbing off on Sean Burke, who has turned in a pair of strong outings after an iffy start to the year, as well as Erick Fedde (3.34 ERA) and Anthony Kay (6.12).</p><p>“He's the perfect example of how you can be successful and pitch deep into games and limit the run production when you're in the zone,” Venable said. “He throws everything for strikes and gets ahead of guys and gets that count leverage.”</p><p>The longest tenured player on the Sox roster, Martin certainly would’ve liked to have gotten to this point earlier. A 14th-round draft pick in 2018, he debuted in 2022 but went down with Tommy John surgery that cost him the ‘23 season and most of ‘24.</p><p>Better late than never for Martin, last year’s staff workhorse (7-10, 4.10 ERA) who entered his second full season with the benefit of his first “full offseason with your arsenal and not having to tweak.”</p><p>“I think at this time last year we were trying to overhaul a slider, and trying to make adjustments in-season in the major leagues is about the hardest thing you can do,” Martin said. “So to have so much confidence in it all going into the season is great.”</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Uber moves toward becoming an 'everything app' with hotel bookings powered by Expedia
entries.summary: <p>Uber is expanding into a different side of the travel business: hotels.</p><p>The ride-hailing and delivery company said Wednesday that users of its app can now book hotel rooms. Uber is using hotel listings provided by Expedia Group, a booking service that works with 700,000 hotels and other properties globally. More than 1 million vacation rentals from Vrbo — which is owned by Seattle-based Expedia — will be added to the app later this year, the company said.</p><p>Sachin Kansal, Uber’s chief product officer, said hotel booking is a big step toward San Francisco-based Uber’s goal of becoming an “everything app” that serves many customer needs. Uber, which was founded in 2009, launched Uber Eats for restaurant deliveries in 2015 and expanded with grocery deliveries in 2020.</p><p>“Consumers are spending too much time coordinating their life, using multiple apps. AI is in the air and they’re all trying to figure out, how does AI help me or does it not help me?” Kansal told The Associated Press. “Our goal with these announcements is to bring everything into one app, to help them save time and to also help them save money.”</p><p>Any Uber app user will be able to make hotel reservations. But Uber One members, who pay $9.99 per month for zero delivery fees and other perks, will get a 20% discount off a rolling list of 10,000 hotels plus 10% back in Uber credits they can use to book rides, Kansal said.</p><p>Kansal said Uber evaluated multiple partners before partnering with Expedia. Uber then spent months integrating Expedia’s technology into its own app. Kansal wouldn’t share the financial terms behind the partnership.</p><p>“They’re very excited because Uber brings a certain user base that is very travel-friendly,” Kansal said. “So I would say it’s going to mutually beneficial for both the parties.”</p><p>Travel is a big part of Uber users’ lives, he said. More than 100 million people use Uber to get to or from an airport each year. And last year, more than 1.5 billion Uber trips took place outside of a rider’s home city.</p><p>The hotel-booking feature was one of several travel-related announcements at Uber’s annual new product event Wednesday. Uber said an upgraded travel mode will help users find restaurants and other points of interest in the cities they visit.</p><p>Uber said users will soon get restaurant recommendations and be able to book tables in its app through OpenTable. Rival delivery service DoorDash recently began offering restaurant reservations in its app after buying hospitality platform SevenRooms.</p><p>Uber said it's also launching a service that will let users order a drink or snack that would be waiting for them when they get picked up by a driver using a premium Uber Black vehicle. The service is set to launch in the coming weeks in Atlanta, Philadelphia, Los Angeles and other cities.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/01/20/uber-lyft-driver-union-illinois-transportation-network-driver-labor-relations-act" >100,000 Uber, Lyft drivers could unionize under bill filed in Springfield</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/13/chicago-taxicab-fares-increase-20-percent-lyft-uber" >Council committee backs raising Chicago cab fares 20% to save taxi industry</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Dee-Ann Durbin | AP

entries.title: Sheridan Gorman's family calls for 'meaningful change' as Loyola student's suspected killer pleads not guilty
entries.summary: <p>Family and friends of Loyola University Chicago first-year student Sheridan Gorman watched Wednesday as the man accused of killing her pleaded not guilty, just over a month after the slaying became a flashpoint in the thorny nationwide debate over immigration enforcement.</p><p>Gorman’s supporters packed a hearing room at the Leighton Criminal Courthouse where <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/ice-detainer-loyola-student-killing-sheridan-gorman" target="_blank" >Jose Medina</a> appeared for his arraignment before Judge Alfredo Maldonado.</p><p>Afterward, the family held up photos of the slain 18-year-old while speaking to local media outlets for the first time since Gorman was killed near a Far North Side beach.</p><p>“It's the beginning of a long process, and our family will be here every single step of the way until there is a full accountability for what was done to our daughter, Sheridan,” her father, Thomas Gorman, told reporters.</p><p>Sheridan Gorman’s mother fought through tears as she thanked “the entire nation” for its support. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Sheridan Gorman, 18, was shot and killed near Loyola University Chicago’s Rogers Park campus last month.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Our daughter was beautiful on the inside and out,” Jessica Gorman said. “She really mattered.” </p><p>Medina, a 26-year-old Venezuelan immigrant, faces first-degree murder, attempted murder and weapons charges for allegedly shooting Gorman in her back as she and her friends were walking on the lakefront in Rogers Park near the Loyola campus last month. </p><p>President Donald Trump and fellow conservative critics <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/24/chicago-crime-trump-pritzker-bovino-immigration-midway-blitz-deport-immigration" target="_blank" >blamed Chicago’s sanctuary policies</a> for the teenager’s death. But Thomas Gorman said the family doesn’t want to see her killing turned into a partisan talking point — they want “meaningful change that puts safety over politics.”</p><p>“We are not interested in slogans,” he said. “We are not interested in political spin. We are interested in only one thing: to make sure that this does not happen to another family.</p><p>“Make no mistake about it, Sheridan's death was not just a senseless tragedy,” he added, “It was preventable.”</p><p>On March 19, Sheridan Gorman and her friends were walking toward the end of the pier at Tobey Prinz Beach when they spotted Medina hiding in the shadows, according to Cook County prosecutors.</p><p>Medina jumped out wearing a mask and holding a gun, prosecutors said. Then he fired a single shot as the group tried to run away, striking Gorman in her back and killing her, they said. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jose Medina has been charged in the killing of Loyola University Chicago student Sheridan Gorman.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Cook County public defender’s office</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Prosecutors said video evidence helped detectives trace Medina back to his nearby apartment. When police searched his unit, they found a gun wrapped in a ski mask near his bed, they said.</p><p>Medina’s public defenders said he <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/27/sheridan-gorman-loyola-murder-jose-medina" target="_blank" >has a developmental disability</a> from an earlier gunshot wound he suffered to his head during a robbery in Colombia. He’d made his way there after fleeing Venezuela.</p><p>Medina ended up in Chicago in 2023 after reaching the U.S.-Mexico border in Texas and being detained there. Even though he requested to be deported back to Colombia, officials in Texas bused him to Chicago, according to his public defenders.</p><p>When he was arrested in connection to Gorman’s killing, Medina had an active warrant for a 2023 shoplifting case. Federal prosecutors have also charged Medina with <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/02/chicago-crime-sheridan-gorman-loyola" target="_blank" >possessing a pistol</a> while being unlawfully present in the United States. U.S. Attorney Andrew S. Boutros said his office was taking “no chances” that Medina would be released. </p><p>Medina remains held at Cook County Jail in the murder case. His next court date in the murder case was set for June 1.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/ice-detainer-loyola-student-killing-sheridan-gorman" target="_blank" >Man charged with murder of Loyola student Sheridan Gorman</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/24/chicago-crime-trump-pritzker-bovino-immigration-midway-blitz-deport-immigration" target="_blank" >Sheridan Gorman’s killing puts Chicago back at the center of America’s bitter fight over immigration</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/27/sheridan-gorman-loyola-murder-jose-medina" target="_blank" >Man charged with Loyola freshman’s murder has disability from gunshot to the head, attorney says</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/02/chicago-crime-sheridan-gorman-loyola" target="_blank" >Feds hit Sheridan Gorman’s alleged killer with gun charge, taking ‘no chances’ he’ll be released</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Panel de responsabilidad pide cargos criminales por mala conducta de agentes durante Operativo Midway Blitz
entries.summary: <p>El presidente de un panel de responsabilidad que examina el impacto del Operativo Midway Blitz instó a la fiscal del estado del Condado de Cook, Eileen O’Neill Burke, a enjuiciar a los agentes federales de inmigración por conducta indebida.</p><p>El juez Rubén Castillo, presidente de la Comisión de Responsabilidad de Illinois, hizo este llamado durante sus comentarios finales en la última audiencia de la comisión el martes. La comisión tenía la tarea de examinar las tácticas utilizadas el otoño pasado durante la campaña de deportación del presidente Donald Trump.</p><p>Un informe final del panel está programado para publicarse el jueves e incluirá recomendaciones específicas para funcionarios locales y estatales, dijo Castillo.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-fb0000" name="module-fb0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>"Nuestro informe va a hacer recomendaciones muy específicas y detalladas dirigidas a fortalecer la responsabilidad y prevenir la reaparición de la devastación comunitaria que ha ocurrido en Illinois y en otras comunidades como Minneapolis", comentó Castillo. "Este informe debe ser evaluado por otros estados y por funcionarios públicos en este estado que puedan tomar las acciones apropiadas, comenzando por la fiscal del estado del Condado de Cook, que recibirá casos para enjuiciamiento criminal".</p><p>Castillo, un exjuez principal del distrito, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/13/coalicion-dice-que-fiscal-del-condado-de-cook-eileen-oneill-burke-ignoro-la-violencia-de-ice" target="_blank" >es parte de la coalición que pide un fiscal especial</a> para investigar a los agentes federales.</p><p>Además, fue más allá el martes y dijo que los miembros de la administración de Trump deberían rendir cuentas, incluyendo al asesor principal Stephen Miller, a la exsecretaria del Departamento de Seguridad (DHS) Nacional Kristi Noem y a Gregory Bovino, oficial de la Patrulla Fronteriza que supervisó el Operativo Midway Blitz y campañas similares en otras ciudades.</p><p>"Ahora realmente depende de nosotros ir tras algunos de estos altos funcionarios que pusieron en marcha estos operativos de campo", dijo Castillo. "Los implementaron intencionalmente y necesitamos actuar contra ellos de la misma manera".</p><p>El informe del jueves también nombrará a agentes federales específicos que la comisión alega que violaron la ley. Uno de esos agentes es Charles Exum, el agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza que le disparó a Marimar Martínez cinco veces después de que un automóvil con tres agentes de la Oficina de Aduanas y Protección Fronteriza (CBP) chocara con su vehículo el 4 de octubre cerca de la 39th Street y la Kedzie Avenue en el vecindario de Brighton Park.</p><p>Martínez y su abogado Christopher Parente testificaron ante la comisión el martes sobre el tiroteo y sus consecuencias. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/04/23/marimar-martinez-testifica-y-exige-responsabilidad-o-mas-personas-resultaran-heridas" target="_blank" >Martínez testificó previamente ante el Congreso</a>. Tras el tiroteo, los fiscales federales acusaron a Martínez, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/11/21/desestiman-el-caso-contra-mujer-a-la-que-le-disparo-agente-de-la-patrulla-fronteriza-en-brighton-park" target="_blank" >pero luego desestimaron el caso en su contra</a>.</p><p>La comisión revisó la grabación de la cámara corporal y el video de vigilancia del día en que le dispararon, así como los registros judiciales relacionados con el caso criminal desestimado.</p><p>"La comisión ha determinado que existe causa razonable para creer que el agente Charles Exum disparó a una civil desarmada sin justificación", dijo el comisionado Jimmy Arce. "Este es un patrón de eventos extraordinario y profundamente alarmante; no son lapsos procedimentales ni errores técnicos, sino violaciones constantes e intencionales de la ley".</p><p>La audiencia del martes incluyó un testimonio pregrabado de una persona anónima que presenció el tiroteo. La persona, que la comisión dijo que era un observador independiente, afirmó que los agentes no estaban bajo ataque ni amenazados por Martínez, pero le dispararon de todos modos.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-620000" name="image-620000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Marimar Martínez, quien sobrevivió tras recibir cinco disparos de un agente federal de inmigración el otoño pasado, muestra una de sus heridas de bala durante una audiencia de la Comisión de Responsabilidad de Illinois en el Loop el martes 28 de abril de 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Después de que le dispararon a Martínez, miembros de la comunidad se reunieron en protesta cerca del lugar del tiroteo. En respuesta, los agentes federales dispararon 15 rondas de gas lacrimógeno y spray de gas pimienta a la multitud, el mayor despliegue de armas químicas en Chicago en 60 años, según la comisión.</p><p>La audiencia también profundizó en otro incidente principal durante el Operativo Midway Blitz <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/10/15/tras-persecucion-en-calle-residencial-de-chicago-agentes-federales-embisten-vehiculo-y-lanzan-gas-lacrimogeno" target="_blank" >en el vecindario de East Side</a> y el tiroteo fatal de <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/09/16/silverio-villegas-gonzalez-quien-fue-asesinado-por-agentes-de-ice-toco-la-vida-de-tantos" target="_blank" >Silverio Villegas González</a> en septiembre pasado en el suburbio de Franklin Park.</p><p>Esos incidentes y los examinados durante la audiencia del lunes fueron "muestras sin precedentes de brutalidad y mostraron fallas repetidas en la rendición de cuentas", dijo Castillo.</p><p>"Como comisión hemos desarrollado un registro de evidencia muy sustancial. En mis 25 años como juez federal, nunca he visto el tipo de brutalidad mostrado por los agentes involucrados en el Operativo Midway Blitz", aseguró Castillo. "Solo podemos esperar y rezar que los cambios que sugerimos se implementen para que esta conducta nunca vuelva a ocurrir en suelo estadounidense".</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: Recortes a la asistencia alimentaria de SNAP preocupan a comerciantes y vendedores locales
entries.summary: <p>Living Fresh Market, una tienda de comestibles independiente en el suburbio del oeste, Forest Park, vio una disminución de sus ingresos y un cambio en su base de clientes el otoño pasado, cuando el cierre del gobierno federal ocasionó una pausa temporal en la asistencia alimentaria federal.</p><p>La tienda de propietarios afroamericanos incluso hizo un llamado público en busca de ayuda de la comunidad. Y ahora, se está preparando para otra disminución de sus ingresos que comenzará en mayo, cuando se espera que miles de personas empiecen a perder los beneficios del Programa de Asistencia Nutricional Suplementaria (SNAP) debido a los nuevos requisitos de trabajo más estrictos.</p><p>Aproximadamente el 15% de los ingresos semanales de la tienda provienen de personas que compran comestibles con beneficios de SNAP, dijo Melody Winston, ejecutiva principal de Living Fresh Market.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-f70000" name="module-f70000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>"Cuando hablamos de lo que ha sucedido debido a la disminución de los beneficios de SNAP, también entendemos que hay un efecto dominó que ocurrirá, no sólo para las grandes [tiendas] sino también para las pequeñas", dijo Winston el lunes. "Las pequeñas son quienes luchan por la comunidad. Las pequeñas son quienes se quedan aquí sin importar cuán ajustados sean los márgenes".</p><p>Los funcionarios de Illinois han estimado que cerca de 150,000 personas perderán beneficios a partir de mayo, a menos que soliciten con éxito una exención o muestren prueba de que están haciendo voluntariado o trabajando 80 horas al mes. Las nuevas reglas laborales, que ahora incluyen a personas de 55 a 64 años junto con padres cuyo hijo menor tiene 14 años o más, entraron en vigor en febrero y son resultado de la amplia reforma económica del presidente Donald Trump que se aprobó el año pasado.</p><p>En Forest Park, los clientes de Living Fresh Market son recibidos con grandes letreros que explican cómo ampliar sus dólares de SNAP a través de un programa llamado Link Match. Winston, de pie frente a una exhibición de manzanas coloridas, fue acompañada por el Greater Chicago Food Depository para advertir sobre los efectos en cadena que tendrá la pérdida de beneficios de SNAP para los dueños de negocios pequeños.</p><p>"Es casi como una tormenta perfecta que está golpeando al país en este momento", dijo Winston. "Y agregar esto encima no va a ayudar a nadie. Van a empeorar las cosas en cada hogar".</p><p>En Illinois, había más de 9,400 minoristas autorizados de SNAP en el año fiscal 2024, según datos del Departamento de Agricultura de los Estados Unidos.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-65c706" name="image-65c706"></a> <picture
